## Summary

In 2024/25, 19.0% of full-time academic staff at the University of Glasgow held teaching-only (LTS) contracts, placing Glasgow 7th among the 22-institution Russell Group comparator (Oxford and Cambridge excluded) and 2.6 percentage points above the peer mean of 16.4%. The LTS share has risen by 9.3 percentage points since 2014/15, broadly in line with sector trends. Higher student-to-staff ratios and higher research-only shares are both positively associated with LTS share across the sector, and Glasgow's profile is consistent with both.

## Data and methods

The primary source is HESA Table 17 (institution-level counts of full-time academic staff by employment function, contract level, sex, and salary band), spanning 2014/15 to 2024/25. Part-time staff are excluded throughout — the most important constraint on interpretation. Staff are included where employment function is Teaching only, Research only, or Both teaching and research, and contract level is Professor, Other senior academic, or Other contract level. The Russell Group comparator comprises 22 institutions; Oxford and Cambridge are excluded as structural outliers. "Russell Group" throughout refers to this 22-institution set.

Student headcount data are from HESA's published provider-level snapshot, used as a contemporaneous approximation for the most recent year. The all-provider analyses exclude the Open University, Arden University, providers with fewer than 1,000 students or fewer than 100 academic staff, and providers with a 0% or 100% teaching-only share.

In 2024/25 Glasgow stood at 19.0%, the Russell Group (excl. Oxford, Cambridge and Glasgow) at 15.6%, and non-Russell Group institutions at 19.9%. All three groups have risen since 2014/15, when Glasgow (9.7%) already sat above the RG peer mean (9.1%) and below non-Russell Group institutions (9.8%). This ordering has held throughout the decade.

At professor level, Glasgow's LTS share (7.2%) is approximately twice that of the RG peer average (3.8%) — the most striking single figure in this breakdown, indicating that LTS appointments extend into senior grades more systematically at Glasgow than at comparable institutions. At other contract level the gap is smaller: Glasgow at 22.3% versus 18.1% for RG peers and 22.3% for non-Russell Group institutions.

Glasgow's elevated LTS share (19.0%) is accompanied by a research-only share (33.9%) similar to the RG peer average (37.4%) and above non-Russell Group institutions (23.4%). The both-teaching-and-research share (47.1%) is close to the RG average (47.1%). A higher LTS share alongside a maintained research-only share is consistent with a segmented staffing model in which dedicated teaching-focused and research-focused roles grow partly at the expense of undifferentiated appointments.

## Synthesis and discussion

Glasgow's LTS share in 2024/25 (19.0%) places it 7th among the 22-institution Russell Group comparator, 2.6 percentage points above the peer mean. This position is consistent across contract levels and most pronounced at professor grade, where Glasgow's LTS share is approximately twice the peer average. The share has grown modestly over the decade, broadly in parallel with the wider sector.

Both student-to-staff ratio and research intensity are positively associated with LTS share within the Russell Group, and Glasgow's profile is consistent with both. The positive correlation between research-only share and LTS share (rho = -0.77) is consistent with a segmented staffing model in which growth in teaching-focused and research-focused roles occurs partly at the expense of undifferentiated teaching-and-research appointments.

The data permit the VP to state that Glasgow's elevated LTS share is consistent with its scale and staffing model, and that the analyses do not establish whether the current level is strategically appropriate. A recommended next step is REF 2021 institution-level research intensity data, which would allow a more direct test of the segmented staffing hypothesis.

## Limitations

These analyses use HESA Table 17, which covers full-time academic staff only; part-time staff are excluded throughout. Because women are more likely to work part-time, the data likely understate the LTS share among female staff.

HESA rounds FPE counts to the nearest 5, introducing noise at the institutional level, particularly for small cells such as the other senior academic contract level (approximately 15 FPE at Glasgow in 2024/25).

Cross-institutional comparison assumes consistent classification of academic employment function. In practice, the boundary between teaching only and both teaching and research may be drawn differently across providers.

The student headcount file is a single snapshot used as a contemporaneous approximation; institutions with atypical year-on-year enrolment changes may be mismeasured relative to their staff complement.

All analyses are descriptive and do not establish causation.
